intro: The nativity of Christ — the birth of Jesus of Nazareth — fulfilled multiple prophetic predictions: that the Messiah would be born of a virgin (Isa. 7:14; Matt. 1:22–23), that his birthplace would be Bethlehem of Judah (Mic. 5:2; Matt. 2:6), and that the flight to Egypt and the slaughter of Bethlehem's children would occur (Hos. 11:1; Jer. 31:15; Matt. 2:15–18). Luke records the birth in a manger because there was no room in the inn (Luke 2:7), the announcement to shepherds by angels, and the visit of the magi (recorded in Matthew 2). The date of the nativity is uncertain; early church fathers proposed dates in spring or autumn, and December 25 was established as the Western liturgical observance by the 4th century. The incarnation — God taking on human nature in Jesus — is theologically central to both soteriology (Christ as the second Adam) and Christology (the hypostatic union of divine and human natures).
